---
title: "Tic-Tac-Toe with RewriteGames.jl"
subtitle: "From algebraic rewriting rules to a trained ONNX policy"
author: "RewriteGames.jl contributors"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: false
    highlight-style: github
    theme: cosmo
jupyter: julia-1.11
execute:
  eval: false        # set to true once dependencies are installed
  echo: true
---

## Overview

**RewriteGames.jl** models game states as *attributed C-sets* (ACSets) and
moves as *algebraic rewrite rules*.  Both concepts come from applied category
theory: ACSets are typed relational data structures whose schemas describe
objects (tables) and morphisms (foreign keys), and rewrite rules describe how
those structures transform under play.

This choice of representation is deliberately general.  Any game whose state
can be described as a relational structure — boards, graphs, maps, unit
networks — fits naturally into the framework.  Because every game shares the
same substrate, the engine's bookkeeping (match enumeration, budget tracking,
serialisation) applies uniformly across games without modification.

Two longer-term goals shape the library's design, though neither is fully
realised yet:

- **Modular game composition.**  ACSets and rewrite rules support algebraic
  composition: schemas can be combined, rules can be glued together, and
  schedules can be nested.  The infrastructure for this — `GameMigration`,
  compositional schedules — is in place; a library of reusable primitives is
  forthcoming.
- **Cross-game transfer.**  Because games share a common representation, a
  policy trained on one game could, in principle, transfer to another via
  *schema migration*.  This is ongoing research; the current library provides
  the representational foundation but not yet the transfer machinery.

In this tutorial we use **tic-tac-toe** to demonstrate every layer of the
framework: simple enough to understand at a glance, rich enough to exercise
the full workflow.

1. Model the board as an ACSet and the legal moves as algebraic rewrite rules.
2. Run self-play episodes and serialise the experience stream to Arrow.
3. Train a policy network in **PyTorch** with REINFORCE.
4. Export the policy to **ONNX** and load it back into Julia as a drop-in agent.

```{julia}
#| label: setup
using Catlab, AlgebraicRewriting, RewriteGames
using Arrow, Tables, Random, Statistics
```

---

## Part 1 — Modelling the Game in Julia

### Schema design

An ACSet (attributed C-set) is a relational data structure whose *schema*
describes the tables and foreign keys.  For tic-tac-toe we use three tables:

| Table | Meaning |
|-------|---------|
| `Sq`  | Board squares (always exactly 9) |
| `X`   | X pieces placed so far |
| `O`   | O pieces placed so far |

Two foreign keys record which square each piece occupies:

```{julia}
#| label: schema
@present SchTTT(FreeSchema) begin
    Sq::Ob
    X::Ob
    O::Ob
    xsq::Hom(X, Sq)
    osq::Hom(O, Sq)
end

@acset_type TicTacToe(SchTTT, index=[:xsq, :osq])
```

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-schema
%%| fig-cap: "Schema for tic-tac-toe: each piece points to the square it occupies."
graph LR
    X["X  (pieces)"] -->|xsq| Sq["Sq  (squares)"]
    O["O  (pieces)"] -->|osq| Sq
```

We index on both foreign keys so that `incident(board, sq_id, :xsq)` — "all X
pieces on square `sq_id`" — is an O(1) lookup.

Squares are numbered 1–9 in row-major order:

```
 1 │ 2 │ 3
───┼───┼───
 4 │ 5 │ 6
───┼───┼───
 7 │ 8 │ 9
```

The initial world has 9 squares and no pieces:

```{julia}
#| label: initial-board
function initial_board()
    b = TicTacToe()
    add_parts!(b, :Sq, 9)
    return b
end
```

### Game schedule

Every game in RewriteGames is driven by a **schedule** — a tree of `GameStep`
nodes that describes one *round* of play.  The driver executes the tree once
per round and loops until the terminal predicate fires or `T_max` steps are
reached.

The schedule determines *who can act and when*.  Each `PlayerStep` asks the
named player's agent to pick from their available rewrite rules and apply one.
Because the schedule is explicit, turn order, conditional branching, and
per-unit actions are all expressible without modifying the engine.

For tic-tac-toe, one round is simply "X moves, then O moves":

```julia
Seq(PlayerStep(:X), PlayerStep(:O))
```

`Seq` runs its children in order and **short-circuits** as soon as the game
ends — if X wins mid-round, O never moves.

The full set of node types is:

| Type | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `PlayerStep(p)` | Player `p` selects and applies one action |
| `AutoStep()` | Fire `game.auto` rules without player input |
| `Seq(steps...)` | Run children in order; short-circuit on terminal |
| `Cond(pred, branches...)` | Branch on `pred(W) -> Int` (1-based index) |
| `WhileStep(cond, body)` | Repeat `body` while `cond(W)` is true |
| `ForEachStep(ob, body)` | Iterate over every part of type `ob` |

Each `Experience` record carries a `schedule_path` vector that identifies
which nodes were active when it was emitted — useful for logging, replay, and
attributing steps in multi-agent or hierarchical schedules.

::: {.callout-note}
`PlayerStep` does **not** automatically fire auto-rules; that is `AutoStep`'s
responsibility.  For games with probabilistic environment events, interleave
`AutoStep()` nodes explicitly in the schedule.  Tic-tac-toe has no auto-rules,
so the two-node `Seq` above is the complete schedule.
:::

### Rewrite rules

In tic-tac-toe, each player's only legal move is to place their piece on an
*empty* square.  We model this as an algebraic rewrite rule on the board ACSet:
find an empty square (the *match*), then add a piece pointing at it (the
*rewrite*).

A rewrite rule in AlgebraicRewriting is a **span** `L ← I → R` of ACSet
morphisms:

- **L** (left): the pattern that must be found in the current world.
- **I** (interface): the sub-pattern of L that is *preserved* by the rewrite.
- **R** (right): what the matched region becomes after the rewrite.

The engine finds all injective morphisms `m : L → G` from the pattern `L`
into the current world `G` that satisfy every attached condition.  For each
valid match it applies the double-pushout (DPO) construction to produce the
updated world `G'`.

#### Pattern and interface

Both the "place X" and "place O" rules search for the same thing: one bare
square.  We build the shared pattern and interface once:

```{julia}
#| label: rules-pattern
# Shared pattern and interface: one bare square
I_sq = @acset TicTacToe begin Sq=1 end
L_sq = @acset TicTacToe begin Sq=1 end

l_sq = ACSetTransformation(I_sq, L_sq; Sq=[1])   # identity on Sq
```

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-rule-span
%%| fig-cap: "Span L ← I → R for placing a piece. L and I are a single bare square; R adds a piece pointing at that square."
graph LR
    subgraph L["L — find a square"]
        LSq["Sq"]
    end
    subgraph I["I — keep the square"]
        ISq["Sq"]
    end
    subgraph R["R — square + new piece"]
        RSq["Sq"]
        RX["X  (or O)"]
        RX -->|"xsq"| RSq
    end
    ISq -.->|l| LSq
    ISq -.->|r| RSq
```

This span alone would match *every* square on the board, including occupied
ones.  We need to restrict matches to squares that are genuinely empty.

#### Negative Application Conditions

A **Negative Application Condition** (NAC) is a morphism `f : L → N` that
forbids matches.  Formally, a match `m : L → G` is only legal if there is *no*
morphism `n : N → G` satisfying `n ∘ f = m`.  In plain English: the engine
checks whether the larger pattern `N` can be embedded in the world consistently
with the proposed match.  If it can, the match is rejected.

For tic-tac-toe, `N` is "a square with a piece already on it".  We build one
NAC forbidding X-occupied squares and one forbidding O-occupied squares:

```{julia}
#| label: rules-nac
# Forbidden patterns: a square that already holds a piece
N_x_occ = @acset TicTacToe begin Sq=1; X=1; xsq=[1] end  # square with an X
N_o_occ = @acset TicTacToe begin Sq=1; O=1; osq=[1] end  # square with an O

# Morphisms send the bare L_sq square to the square inside each forbidden pattern
nac_f_x = ACSetTransformation(L_sq, N_x_occ; Sq=[1])
nac_f_o = ACSetTransformation(L_sq, N_o_occ; Sq=[1])

nac_no_x = NAC(nac_f_x)   # reject if matched square already holds an X
nac_no_o = NAC(nac_f_o)   # reject if matched square already holds an O
```

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-nac
%%| fig-cap: "NAC for an X-occupied square. If the candidate match m : L → G extends through f to a morphism n : N → G, the match is rejected."
graph LR
    subgraph L["L — candidate square"]
        LSq["Sq"]
    end
    subgraph N["N — forbidden: square with X"]
        NSq["Sq"]
        NX["X"]
        NX -->|xsq| NSq
    end
    LSq -.->|f| NSq
```

Attaching both `nac_no_x` and `nac_no_o` to a rule means `get_matches`
automatically returns only squares that are empty in both respects.  The game
engine never sees an illegal move candidate; no post-hoc filtering is needed.

#### Completing the rules

With the shared interface and NACs in hand, building the two rules is
straightforward:

```{julia}
#| label: rules-complete
# Place X on the matched square
R_x  = @acset TicTacToe begin Sq=1; X=1; xsq=[1] end
r_x  = ACSetTransformation(I_sq, R_x; Sq=[1])
rule_place_x = Rule(l_sq, r_x; ac=[nac_no_x, nac_no_o])

# Place O on the matched square
R_o  = @acset TicTacToe begin Sq=1; O=1; osq=[1] end
r_o  = ACSetTransformation(I_sq, R_o; Sq=[1])
rule_place_o = Rule(l_sq, r_o; ac=[nac_no_x, nac_no_o])
```

Both rules forbid playing on a square that holds *either* piece — only a
square that is empty in both respects produces a valid match.

### Terminal predicate

We check the eight winning lines (3 rows, 3 columns, 2 diagonals):

```{julia}
#| label: terminal
const WINS = [
    [1,2,3], [4,5,6], [7,8,9],    # rows
    [1,4,7], [2,5,8], [3,6,9],    # columns
    [1,5,9], [3,5,7],             # diagonals
]

function check_terminal(board)
    xs = Set(subpart(board, parts(board, :X), :xsq))
    os = Set(subpart(board, parts(board, :O), :osq))

    for line in WINS
        all(s ∈ xs for s in line) && return (true, :X)
        all(s ∈ os for s in line) && return (true, :O)
    end

    nparts(board, :X) + nparts(board, :O) == 9 && return (true, nothing)
    return (false, nothing)
end
```

### Assembling the game with `@game`

RewriteGames exports a `@game` DSL macro that builds a `Game` from a concise
block.  Keyword clauses map to the `Game(...)` constructor arguments:

```{julia}
#| label: game-macro
# No post_filter needed — the NACs embedded in the rules already ensure that
# get_matches only returns empty-square matches.
#
# RuleEntry also accepts an optional `budget=N` keyword that caps how many
# times the rule may fire per episode.  Tic-tac-toe needs no cap (each player
# can place at most 5 pieces naturally), but it is useful for more complex
# games where rule-use limits encode game semantics.
entry_x = RuleEntry(rule_place_x; name=:place)
entry_o = RuleEntry(rule_place_o; name=:place)

game = @game nothing begin
    players:  X, O
    X:        [entry_x]
    O:        [entry_o]
    terminal: check_terminal
    initial:  initial_board
    schedule: Seq(PlayerStep(:X), PlayerStep(:O))
end
```

::: {.callout-note}
`initial: initial_board` passes the function object itself (no parentheses).
The engine calls `game.initial()` at the start of each episode.

`schedule: Seq(PlayerStep(:X), PlayerStep(:O))` makes the round structure
explicit.  Without a `schedule:` clause the engine auto-generates
`Seq(PlayerStep(p), AutoStep())` for each player in order, which is equivalent
for this game — but supplying the schedule explicitly is clearer and is the
recommended style.
:::

### Displaying the board

A quick ASCII helper returns the board as a string:

```{julia}
#| label: display
function show_board(board) :: String
    marks = fill(' ', 3, 3)
    for xp in parts(board, :X)
        sq = subpart(board, xp, :xsq)
        marks[(sq-1)÷3+1, (sq-1)%3+1] = 'X'
    end
    for op in parts(board, :O)
        sq = subpart(board, op, :osq)
        marks[(sq-1)÷3+1, (sq-1)%3+1] = 'O'
    end
    sep = "───┼───┼───"
    rows = String[]
    for (i, row) in enumerate(eachrow(marks))
        push!(rows, " " * join(string.(row), " │ "))
        i < 3 && push!(rows, sep)
    end
    return join(rows, "\n")
end
```

### Running a quick random episode

```{julia}
#| label: random-episode
Random.seed!(42)

random_agents = Dict{Symbol, AbstractAgent}(
    :X => FunctionAgent((state, actions) -> rand(actions)),
    :O => FunctionAgent((state, actions) -> rand(actions)),
)

exps = run_game(game, random_agents; T_max=20)

println("Episode: $(length(exps)) steps")
println("Winner:  $(exps[end].winner)")
println()

print(show_board(exps[end].next_state.raw.world))
```

You should see output like:

```
Episode: 9 steps
Winner:  X

 X │ O │ X
───┼───┼───
 O │ X │ O
───┼───┼───
   │ O │ X
```

We can also compute quick statistics with the analysis helpers:

```{julia}
#| label: quick-stats
# Pool many episodes
all_episodes = [run_game(game, random_agents; T_max=20) for _ in 1:200]
all_exps     = reduce(vcat, all_episodes)

println("X win rate:          ", round(win_rate(all_exps, :X); digits=3))
println("O win rate:          ", round(win_rate(all_exps, :O); digits=3))
println("Mean episode length: ", round(mean(episode_length, all_episodes); digits=1))
println("Action counts:       ", action_counts(all_exps))
```

---

## Part 2 — Collecting Training Data

Before training in Python we need to serialise the board state and chosen action
for every step.

::: {.callout-note}
### Built-in vs custom serialisation

RewriteGames exports `write_experiences` / `read_experiences` (backed by
Arrow) that save the *generic* ACSet encoding from every `Experience` —
`node_features`, `edge_index`, rule counters, and the chosen rule name.  This
is convenient for graph-neural-network policies that consume the raw ACSet
graph.

Here we write a **game-specific** writer instead, because our policy network
expects a fixed 27-element one-hot feature vector (9 squares × 3 states) that
is cheaper and more interpretable than the generic encoding.  Whenever your
policy can work with the generic ACSet encoding, prefer `write_experiences`.
:::

### Feature extraction

We represent each board as a 9 × 3 one-hot matrix (rows = squares, columns =
[empty, X, O]) and flatten it to a 27-element `Float32` vector:

```{julia}
#| label: features
function board_features(board) :: Vector{Float32}
    feats = zeros(Float32, 9, 3)
    xs = Set(subpart(board, parts(board, :X), :xsq))
    os = Set(subpart(board, parts(board, :O), :osq))
    for sq in 1:9
        if sq ∈ xs
            feats[sq, 2] = 1f0
        elseif sq ∈ os
            feats[sq, 3] = 1f0
        else
            feats[sq, 1] = 1f0
        end
    end
    return vec(feats)
end
```

### Custom experience writer

We collect `(features, chosen_square, player, outcome)` tuples across many
episodes and write them to an Arrow file:

```{julia}
#| label: data-collection
struct StepRecord
    features       :: Vector{Float32}  # 27-element board encoding
    chosen_square  :: Int32            # 1–9; 0 means the player passed
    player         :: String           # "X" or "O"
    outcome        :: Float32          # +1 win, -1 loss, 0 draw/timeout
end

function collect_episodes(game, agents; n_episodes=5_000, T_max=20)
    records = StepRecord[]

    for _ in 1:n_episodes
        episode = run_game(game, agents; T_max=T_max)
        final   = episode[end]
        winner  = final.winner       # :X, :O, or nothing

        for exp in episode
            exp.action === nothing && continue   # skip passes

            sq    = Int32(components(exp.action.match)[:Sq][1])
            board = exp.state.raw.world
            feats = board_features(board)
            out   = if winner === exp.player; 1f0
                    elseif winner === nothing;  0f0
                    else                       -1f0 end

            push!(records, StepRecord(feats, sq, string(exp.player), out))
        end
    end
    return records
end
```

Run the collector with X playing randomly and O playing randomly, then save:

```{julia}
#| label: save-data
Random.seed!(0)
records = collect_episodes(game, random_agents; n_episodes=10_000)

# Flatten into columnar Arrow format
Arrow.write(
    "ttt_training.arrow",
    (
        features      = [r.features      for r in records],
        chosen_square = [r.chosen_square for r in records],
        player        = [r.player        for r in records],
        outcome       = [r.outcome       for r in records],
    ),
)
println("Saved $(length(records)) step records to ttt_training.arrow")
```

---

## Part 3 — Training a Policy Network in PyTorch

### Data loading

```{python}
#| label: py-imports
import numpy as np
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torch.distributions import Categorical
import pyarrow as pa
import pyarrow.ipc as ipc
```

```{python}
#| label: py-load-data
def load_training_data(path: str):
    """Load the Arrow file written by Julia."""
    with ipc.open_file(path) as reader:
        tbl = reader.read_all()

    features = np.stack(tbl["features"].to_pylist()).astype(np.float32)  # (N, 27)
    squares  = np.array(tbl["chosen_square"].to_pylist(), dtype=np.int64) - 1  # 0-indexed
    players  = np.array(tbl["player"].to_pylist())
    outcomes = np.array(tbl["outcome"].to_pylist(), dtype=np.float32)

    return features, squares, players, outcomes

features, squares, players, outcomes = load_training_data("ttt_training.arrow")

print(f"Loaded {len(features)} step records")
print(f"Feature shape: {features.shape}")
print(f"X win rate:  {outcomes[players == 'X'].mean():.3f}")
print(f"O win rate:  {outcomes[players == 'O'].mean():.3f}")
```

### Policy network

The network takes the 27-dimensional board encoding and outputs a logit for each
of the 9 squares.  The agent will *mask* the illegal squares (already occupied)
at inference time — the network never needs to worry about them during training
either, since the data only contains legal moves.

```{python}
#| label: py-network
class TicTacToeNet(nn.Module):
    """MLP policy: board features (27) → square logits (9)."""

    def __init__(self, hidden: int = 128):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(27, hidden),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, hidden),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, 9),
        )

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        # x: (batch, 27)  →  logits: (batch, 9)
        return self.net(x)
```

### REINFORCE training loop

We use the Monte Carlo policy-gradient (REINFORCE) algorithm.  Every recorded
step carries its final outcome as the return signal, so the update is simply:

$$\nabla_\theta J \approx \sum_t G_t \, \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)$$

where $G_t \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$ is the game result for the player who moved at
step $t$.

```{python}
#| label: py-train
def train(
    model: nn.Module,
    features: np.ndarray,
    squares: np.ndarray,
    outcomes: np.ndarray,
    *,
    epochs: int = 80,
    batch_size: int = 512,
    lr: float = 3e-4,
    baseline: bool = True,          # subtract a running mean baseline
):
    optimiser = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=lr)
    n = len(features)
    running_baseline = 0.0

    for epoch in range(epochs):
        idx = np.random.permutation(n)
        epoch_loss = 0.0
        batches    = 0

        for start in range(0, n, batch_size):
            batch_idx = idx[start : start + batch_size]
            x   = torch.from_numpy(features[batch_idx])
            a   = torch.from_numpy(squares [batch_idx])
            G   = torch.from_numpy(outcomes[batch_idx])

            if baseline:
                with torch.no_grad():
                    running_baseline = 0.9 * running_baseline + 0.1 * G.mean().item()
                G = G - running_baseline

            logits = model(x)                    # (batch, 9)
            log_prob = F.log_softmax(logits, dim=-1)
            chosen_lp = log_prob[torch.arange(len(a)), a]  # log π(aₜ | sₜ)

            loss = -(chosen_lp * G).mean()       # negative for gradient ascent

            optimiser.zero_grad()
            loss.backward()
            optimiser.step()

            epoch_loss += loss.item()
            batches    += 1

        if (epoch + 1) % 10 == 0:
            print(f"Epoch {epoch+1:3d}  loss={epoch_loss/batches:.4f}")

    return model


torch.manual_seed(0)
model = TicTacToeNet(hidden=128)
model = train(model, features, squares, outcomes, epochs=80)
```

### Evaluating the trained policy

We run a quick greedy evaluation against a random opponent, applying the
illegality mask:

```{python}
#| label: py-eval
def greedy_move(model, features_np, legal_mask):
    """Return the argmax-policy square index (0-indexed), masking illegal squares."""
    x = torch.from_numpy(features_np[None, :])
    with torch.no_grad():
        logits = model(x).squeeze(0)            # (9,)
    logits[~legal_mask] = -1e9                  # mask occupied squares
    return int(logits.argmax())


def evaluate(model, n_games: int = 500, seed: int = 1):
    """
    Play `n_games` of X (policy) vs O (random).
    Returns (x_wins, draws, o_wins).
    """
    rng = np.random.default_rng(seed)
    x_wins = draws = o_wins = 0

    for _ in range(n_games):
        board = np.zeros(9, dtype=np.int8)   # 0=empty, 1=X, 2=O

        for turn in range(9):
            player = 1 if turn % 2 == 0 else 2   # X=1, O=2 moves first
            empty  = np.where(board == 0)[0]

            if player == 1:
                # one-hot features: col0=empty, col1=X, col2=O
                feats = np.zeros(27, dtype=np.float32)
                for sq in range(9):
                    feats[sq * 3 + board[sq]] = 1.0
                mask = torch.zeros(9, dtype=torch.bool)
                mask[empty] = True
                sq = greedy_move(model, feats, mask)
            else:
                sq = int(rng.choice(empty))

            board[sq] = player

            # Check win
            wins = [(0,1,2),(3,4,5),(6,7,8),
                    (0,3,6),(1,4,7),(2,5,8),
                    (0,4,8),(2,4,6)]
            for a,b,c in wins:
                if board[a] == board[b] == board[c] == player:
                    if player == 1: x_wins += 1
                    else:           o_wins += 1
                    board = None   # signal game over
                    break
            if board is None:
                break
        else:
            draws += 1

    return x_wins, draws, o_wins


x_wins, draws, o_wins = evaluate(model, n_games=500)
print(f"X (policy) wins: {x_wins/5:.1f}%  "
      f"Draws: {draws/5:.1f}%  "
      f"O (random) wins: {o_wins/5:.1f}%")
```

### Exporting to ONNX

```{python}
#| label: py-export-onnx
import torch.onnx

def export_onnx(model: nn.Module, path: str = "ttt_policy.onnx"):
    model.eval()
    dummy = torch.zeros(1, 27)   # single-board batch

    torch.onnx.export(
        model,
        dummy,
        path,
        export_params   = True,
        opset_version   = 17,
        do_constant_folding = True,
        input_names     = ["board"],
        output_names    = ["logits"],
        dynamic_axes    = {"board":  {0: "batch"},
                           "logits": {0: "batch"}},
    )
    print(f"ONNX model written to {path}")

export_onnx(model)
```

You can verify the exported graph with `onnxruntime`:

```{python}
#| label: py-verify-onnx
import onnxruntime as ort

sess    = ort.InferenceSession("ttt_policy.onnx")
dummy   = np.zeros((1, 27), dtype=np.float32)
logits, = sess.run(["logits"], {"board": dummy})
print("Output shape:", logits.shape)   # should be (1, 9)
```

---

## Part 4 — Loading the ONNX Policy in Julia

Now we load `ttt_policy.onnx` back into Julia using the `ONNXAgentExt` package
extension.  The extension is activated automatically when you load `ONNXRunTime`
alongside `RewriteGames`.

### `EncodedState` — what agents receive

Every agent's `select_action` method receives an `EncodedState`, which bundles
the raw game state with pre-computed tensor encodings:

| Field | Type | Contents |
|-------|------|----------|
| `raw` | `GameState` | Raw world ACSet plus counters and turn number |
| `node_features` | `Matrix{Float32}` (n × F) | One-hot table type + attribute values per (table, row) node |
| `edge_index` | `Matrix{Int32}` (2 × E) | COO-format foreign-key edges |
| `edge_type` | `Vector{Int8}` | Schema morphism index per edge |
| `rule_counters` | `Vector{Int32}` | Remaining budget per rule |
| `turn_frac` | `Float32` | `turn / T_max` |

`state.raw.world` is the live ACSet.  `state.node_features` and
`state.edge_index` are already in a format suitable for graph neural networks.
Simple agents that only need the raw board can access `state.raw.world`
directly and pay no encoding cost.

### Building the input function

`ONNXAgent` calls `input_fn(state::EncodedState)` before every inference step.
For tic-tac-toe we ignore the generic ACSet encoding and instead build our
fixed-size feature vector directly from the raw board:

```{julia}
#| label: input-fn
function ttt_input_fn(state::EncodedState)
    board = state.raw.world
    feats = board_features(board)              # 27-element Float32 vector
    return Dict("board" => reshape(feats, 1, :))  # shape (1, 27) as expected by ONNX
end
```

### A square-aware policy wrapper

RewriteGames exports `ONNXAgent` as a built-in agent type for models whose
output logits align directly with positions in `legal_actions`.  Tic-tac-toe
does not fit that pattern because our model outputs one logit per **square**
(1–9), while `legal_actions` is a variable-length vector of only the *empty*
squares.  We therefore write a thin `FunctionAgent` wrapper that translates
square logits into the correct `Action`:

```{julia}
#| label: onnx-agent
using ONNXRunTime

function make_ttt_agent(model_path::String)
    session = ONNXRunTime.load_inference(model_path)

    policy = function (state::EncodedState, legal_actions::Vector{Action})
        # Run ONNX inference
        inputs  = ttt_input_fn(state)
        outputs = session(inputs)
        logits  = vec(first(values(outputs)))   # length-9 Float32 vector

        # Map each legal action to its square, pick the highest-logit legal square
        legal_sq  = [components(a.match)[:Sq][1] for a in legal_actions]
        best_idx  = argmax([logits[sq] for sq in legal_sq])
        return legal_actions[best_idx]
    end

    return FunctionAgent(policy)
end

trained_X = make_ttt_agent("ttt_policy.onnx")
```

### Benchmark: trained X vs random O

```{julia}
#| label: benchmark
function benchmark(game, x_agent, o_agent; n=500, T_max=20, seed=1)
    Random.seed!(seed)
    agents = Dict{Symbol, AbstractAgent}(:X => x_agent, :O => o_agent)
    episodes = [run_game(game, agents; T_max=T_max) for _ in 1:n]
    all_exps = reduce(vcat, episodes)

    x_wr = win_rate(all_exps, :X)
    o_wr = win_rate(all_exps, :O)
    draw = 1 - x_wr - o_wr
    println("Over $n games:")
    println("  X (trained) wins: $(round(100x_wr; digits=1))%")
    println("  draws:            $(round(100draw;  digits=1))%")
    println("  O (random) wins:  $(round(100o_wr; digits=1))%")
end

benchmark(game, trained_X, random_agents[:O])
```

Expected output (after sufficient training):

```
Over 500 games:
  X (trained) wins: 74.3%
  draws:            18.1%
  O (random) wins:   7.6%
```

### Watching a game

```{julia}
#| label: watch-game
Random.seed!(7)
demo_exps = run_game(
    game,
    Dict{Symbol, AbstractAgent}(:X => trained_X, :O => random_agents[:O]);
    T_max=20,
)

println("Move-by-move replay\n")
for (i, exp) in enumerate(demo_exps)
    sq = exp.action === nothing ? "–" : string(components(exp.action.match)[:Sq][1])
    println("Turn $i: $(exp.player) plays square $sq")
    print(show_board(exp.next_state.raw.world))
    exp.done && println("\nResult: $(something(exp.winner, :draw))")
    println()
end
```

---

## Summary

| Step | Tool |
|------|------|
| Board state | ACSet with `Sq`, `X`, `O` objects and two foreign-key morphisms |
| Legal moves | `rule_place_{x,o}` with `NAC` conditions banning occupied squares |
| Win detection | `check_terminal` via `incident` on the indexed foreign keys |
| Game loop | `run_game` + `ScheduledGameDriver` via `Seq(PlayerStep(:X), PlayerStep(:O))` |
| Data format | Custom Arrow writer (27-D feature + chosen square + outcome) |
| Training | PyTorch MLP with REINFORCE |
| Bridge | `torch.onnx.export` → `ONNXRunTime.load_inference` → `FunctionAgent` |

RewriteGames.jl handles the combinatorial bookkeeping (pattern matching,
budgets, auto-rules, serialisation) so you can focus on game semantics and
learning algorithms.

### Going further

- **Self-play curriculum**: use a `FunctionAgent` that randomly mixes the
  trained policy with random moves, then progressively reduce the random fraction.
- **Value network**: add a value head to the network for actor-critic methods;
  the `EncodedState.node_features` tensor from RewriteGames is already
  GNN-compatible (COO format + node features).
- **More complex games**: the same schema pattern extends to hex, go, or any
  game whose state is naturally a graph — the rewrite-rule formulation handles
  arbitrary topology changes.
- **Rich schedules**: use `Cond`, `WhileStep`, or `ForEachStep` in the schedule
  tree to model games with variable turn order, per-unit actions (e.g. each
  piece moves independently), or conditional phases.  The `schedule_path` in
  each `Experience` record tells you exactly where in the tree that step was
  emitted.
- **Auto-rules**: wrap any rule in `AutoRule(rule; prob_attr=:p)` and add it
  to the `auto:` clause of `@game`.  Auto-rules fire automatically between
  player steps via `AutoStep()` nodes in the schedule, making it easy to model
  stochastic environment dynamics (weather, spawning, decay).
- **Schema migration**: `GameMigration` lets you apply a Catlab schema functor
  to an entire game (world, rules, and initial factory) in one call.  This is
  useful when you want to extend a prototype schema with new attributes or
  objects without rewriting all the rules from scratch.
